Okay. First of all, I'd like to thank the minister for turning up again
today. We've had you the other day on a different matter.
Also, I'd like to acknowledge the commissioner, Richard Chambers.
You're in London, I think. We're aware that you're going overseas,

conference.

We've got two hours on police this morning, but we've got an
hour and a half with you, Minister.
I'm going to let you do some introductory comments, and then obviously we'll get
into it.
Well, yeah, thank you, Mr. Chair, and thank you to you and the committee.
I'll allow the, I've got the leadership team here from DC to allow
go introduce yourself and from the police and,
obviously Mark Mitchell, Minister of Police and also Minister
for the Serious Fraud Office. I've got a very short opening
comments. I'll pass the others to introduce themselves.
I'm the Deputy Commissioner, Mike Pennant.
Good.
Hi. Good morning. Andrea Conlon, Chief Operating Officer, Police.
Karen Chang, Director of the Serious Fraud Office.
Cool.
The government's expectations have been for police to focus on core policing,
hold offenders to account,
and support victims whose sense of safety and security has
been robbed by crime.
We have put in the resources, investment, and the laws needed to back up our
expectations, and that focus is working.
Wherever I go, people tell me that they see their local police out and about on the
streets,
and they feel safer as a result.
Major centers and urban areas have beat teams and gang disruption units.
The Gangs Act has been a major success in giving police the powers to
ensure gangs no longer have license to intimidate and take
over our provincial towns.
Ram raids have plummeted and retail crime is taken seriously,
benefiting workers, businesses, and the general public.
Boy racers no longer have it easy, and legislation before Parliament will
give police more powers to deal with their reckless and antisocial
behavior on our roads. Police have made excellent
progress on the key government targets that should continue into the coming
year.
Serious and repeat youth offending is down by 25% compared to
2023, and there were 49,000 fewer victims of violent
crime in the year to October 2025 than two years
previously.
As a result, people feel safer. Law and order has dropped well down the list of
issues that are a concern to the public in recent surveys such as
Ipsos that has been shown.
That has not happened by accident.
That is a result of excellent work by police,
as well as this government's investment to ensure the frontline has the
resources and powers to deliver on it.
That support has continued in this year's budget, which delivers
391 million in further investment for police to support the
frontline and the communities that they serve.
Budget 2026 includes investments in technology, property,
and 53.5 million funding for cost pressures to support the
frontline.
The government is delivering on upgrading and replacing stations that our frontline
police across the regions need.
I went to the opening of the new Taradale Police Station earlier this month, a base
that was built on time, under budget, and allows police to better
service the wider Hawke's Bay region.
This year's budget delivers funding for further much-needed replacements for the
Whanganui Police Station and Greymouth Police Station and
custody facility, and that is good news both for the police and the public in
those areas.
The budget also delivers funding to replace the automated biometric
identification system. That is key technology which is crucial for many
areas of core policing. The replacement technology for dealing with biometric
information such as fingerprints will be more effective, more efficient,
and result in faster outcomes for victims.
The budget also delivers on the coalition agreement by providing
8.3 million of funding for those groups which do a great
job in our communities to help the police keep people safe.
This funding for community patrols, Neighborhood Support NZ, and Pacific
Wardens gives them certainty and allows them to continue and expand their
services.
There is still much work to do, but I want to acknowledge the commissioner for his
work over the last year, his support for the frontline, his
focus on building trust and confidence by ensuring police deliver for
victims and the public. It is well-known police has had to deal with
some challenges over the last year.
The response to those challenges, in particular those highlighted in the
IPCA's November 2025 report, will continue to be
a focus for the government. I'm confident police is on solid
footing to address those issues. The new executive and leadership
teams are now in place. I welcome the commissioner's work to strengthen
integrity and his targets around trust and confidence,
public place violence, retail crime resolutions, and the service
people get from police.
The performance improvement review requested by the commissioner has given police
an independent overview of the challenges, and useful guidance on what
is needed over the short and longer terms.
There is good awareness of the challenges that lie ahead and a plan for the future.
In particular, the intended police capability plan is set to
be a valuable exercise in planning for the future investments police
require. The government will continue to work with police to ensure it is
well-placed to meet those challenges. Thank you.
Right. Thank you. I'm going to start with Honorable Jenny Anderson.
Thank you very much, Mr. Chair, and to Minister and police
staff.
Minister, when are you going to reach the 500 more police that you promised at the
last election?
Well, the police are doing an outstanding job.
They've got a really strong pipeline of people wanting to join the police.
We've got people coming back from overseas,
and the numbers that I see and that are being tracked by both myself and
my Associate Minister of Police, Casey Costello, shows that the police
are on target to deliver the 500 towards the end of this year.
You've given lots of other dates in the past.
Why won't you give the public one now?
Well, I've been very clear that the police are showing
a clear motivation to deliver on that target and,
especially with the opening of the new training facility in Auckland,
they've got a strong pipeline of new police officers,
new people wanting to join the police.
We've got a large number of people wanting to rejoin the police, which I take as a
very good signal, and we've got people coming back from Australia.
Why did you promise 500 more police in two years then?
Because that was a coalition commitment.
Right.
Do you think that the police wages are a factor in why you are struggling to
recruit? And if so, are you looking to repay the risk,
as many police are asking?
Well, on a personal level, I always want to see any of our first
responders
paid as much as we can afford as a country.
There is currently bargaining underway, and it would be totally inappropriate for
me to comment on that.
So all members of Parliament have received numerous submissions from frontline
police. I'd just like to read one out of the hundreds we've got.
Quote, "We are increasingly understaffed, routinely exposed to serious
violence, and expected to absorb the cost of that risk.
Like many colleagues, I work long hours, take danger home with me, and still
struggle with the cost of living. The reality is pay no longer reflects
what we are being asked to do. Will you show up for us?
Will you repay the risk?" Do you have a response to that, Minister?
Yes. I'm very proud of the work that our frontline police officers do.
I'm very
aware of the challenges that they have, and that's why this government has invested
enormous amounts of money into our police and into our frontline to support them,
to give them more powers.
Since we've come into government, there's at least an additional 250 police
officers already out there on the front line.
We have freed up police officers from being mental health workers.
So the police are in a very good position in terms of front line
strength. There's more police to come, and there's been an enormous investment into
the front line. When I became police minister, the police were going broke.
The previous government had not invested in their cost pressures, they had not
invested in their core, and they were in danger of breaching their
appropriation. We immediately invested $120 million into police
to stabilize them. And this government is a law and order government, and it
remains committed to continuing to invest, and that's exactly what we've done.
I'm pleased you raised that, Minister, because you might like to explain to the
public why, for the second budget in a row, you appear to be paying for
police wages out of time-limited funding.
Why haven't you baselined the amount of money required to pay the wages
of front line police?
No, we've provided money in this budget for
collective bargaining, which is underway, and it would be totally inappropriate for
me to comment or talk to that.
So I've received that information through written PQs from you that the
$50 million time-limited funding specified in the budget,
$30 million is earmarked for wages out of that.
Why have you not baselined the amount of money required to pay the wages of front
Well, I'm very clear that we have provided money in this budget for
collective bargaining, and it is up to police then to enter into goodwill
collective bargaining with the association and the police guild, and that is
underway at the moment, and it would be totally inappropriate for me to comment any
further on that.
I'm not asking you to comment on the bargaining.

No, that's it. This is last one, Jenny.
You got a question or is that-

Thanks, Chair

so that is what really what the move on orders are there for, so that we
